project.title: Examining an LLM-Based Privacy Warning System for Real-Time Personal Information Disclosure in Encrypted Messaging
project.mission: Design, build, and evaluate a privacy-enhancing system that helps smartphone users recognize and mitigate risks of voluntarily disclosing PII during conversations on encrypted messaging apps.

background.problem: Users of E2EE messaging apps (e.g., WhatsApp) have a false sense of absolute security. E2EE protects data in transit but does not prevent voluntary disclosure of sensitive information, especially under social engineering attacks.
background.gap: Existing privacy tools focus on static analysis or post-hoc detection. There is no real-time, explainable, user-centric support that operates at the moment of message composition.
background.opportunity: LLMs enable context-aware, nuanced understanding of language, allowing for human-in-the-loop (HITL) privacy warnings that inform without restricting user autonomy.

system.concept: A privacy-preserving intervention that analyzes text as users type. It does NOT block messages but provides warnings, explanations, and optional safer rewrites.

design_goals.DG1: Support in the Moment: Provide feedback while composing, not after sending.
design_goals.DG2: Preserve User Autonomy: Inform, don't enforce. User has final say.
design_goals.DG3: Contextual & Explainable: Explain why something is risky in context.
design_goals.DG4: Minimize Disruption: Integrate seamlessly to prevent warning fatigue.

architecture.layer_1: Screen Reading: On-device capture of active chat text.
architecture.layer_2: NER (GLiNER): Local model masks PII before LLM processing.
architecture.layer_3: LLM Risk Assessment: Chain-of-Thought prompting for risk level, rationale, and safer rewrite.
architecture.layer_4: User Interface: Non-blocking inline warning with two options (proceed or accept rewrite).

study.objective: Empirically evaluate the system's usability and effectiveness with real users in a controlled environment.
research_questions: How do users understand AI-generated privacy warnings?
Does the system improve users' ability to notice PII disclosure risks?
How do users perceive usability, trustworthiness, and impact on messaging?
study.hypothesis: Participants using the system will demonstrate higher awareness of PII disclosure risks and report acceptable usability levels.

conditions.group_a: System ON: AI privacy warnings enabled

procedure: 3 fictional messaging scenarios (adapted from SEConvo dataset)
Post-scenario questions on confidence and risk perception
study.key_safeguard: No real personal data used. All scenarios are fictional and pre-seeded.

theoretical_framework.contextual_integrity: Nissenbaum (2004): Information flow is appropriate when it conforms to contextual norms.
theoretical_framework.xai: Miller (2019, 2023): Explanations should be concise, selective, and support decision-making.
theoretical_framework.usable_security: Static warnings lead to habituation; non-blocking, contextual feedback is preferred.

scenarios.description: 1,400 synthetic professional networking conversations simulating CSE attacks
characteristics: No single message appears dangerous alone

system_vs_study.study: The experiment evaluating the prototype (research methodology)

limitations: NER may miss some PII spans
Remote LLM introduces latency and privacy concerns
Fictional scenarios may not generalize to real-world
instructions_for_ai: Adhere to the four design goals in all prototype work.
Respect the between-subjects study design when analyzing data.
Differentiate between the system (prototype) and the study (experiment).
Base risk assessment logic on Contextual Integrity theory.
Prioritize clear, concise explanations aligned with XAI principles.
